title: Danish pastry with apricot and custard
subtitle: Laminated pastry squares filled with pastry cream and tinned apricot
excerpt: Danish pastries use the same laminated enriched dough as croissants, folded and shaped into open squares or pinwheels filled with vanilla pastry cream and topped with half an apricot. The layering gives the pastry its flakiness; the enriched dough gives it a softer, more tender character than a classic French croissant.
sourceNotes: Viennese-style laminated pastries were introduced to Denmark in the mid-nineteenth century by Austrian bakers, where they were adapted with local enriched doughs and custard fillings. The Danish pastry form is now established internationally. The technique here follows standard Danish lamination practice.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked pastries once cool. Reheat at 180 degrees C (conventional) for 8 minutes from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The laminated dough can be made up to 2 days ahead and kept refrigerated. Shape and bake on the day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 180 degrees C; rotate the tray at the 12-minute mark

glossaryTerms.definition: Danish pastry dough is enriched with egg and a higher ratio of sugar than a croissant dough, giving the finished pastry a softer, more bread-like interior alongside the flaky layers. The lamination process is the same: a cold butter block is enclosed in the dough and rolled and folded through three letter folds. Danish pastry is typically given three folds rather than the four or five used in puff pastry, resulting in 27 distinct layers.

content.text: Danish pastries use the same technique as pain aux raisins: an enriched laminated dough, folded three times, proved once cut and shaped. The

produces a slightly softer result than a croissant because the dough contains more sugar and egg. The shaping here is a simple square pinwheel: the pastry cream sits in the centre, the apricot half sits on top, and the four corners fold inward slightly. It is not complicated to shape once the dough is made.

items.prepNote: tinned, halved, drained on kitchen paper

content.text: Dough, pastry cream, and lamination (day one)

Mix the flour, sugar, salt, and yeast. Add the warm milk and eggs and knead for 8 minutes to a smooth dough. Add the soft 40 g butter and knead in for 3 minutes more. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Make the pastry cream: bring the milk just to the boil. Whisk the yolks, sugar, and flour together until pale. Pour the hot milk over slowly, whisk, return to the pan, and stir over medium heat until thick and glossy. Stir in the vanilla. Press cling film onto the surface and refrigerate. Beat the cold lamination butter between two sheets of cling film to a 20 cm square. Roll the dough to a 40 cm square, enclose the butter block, and roll and fold three times with a 30-minute refrigerator rest between each fold. Refrigerate overnight after the final fold.

Roll the cold dough to a 60 cm by 40 cm rectangle and cut into 12 squares of about 10 cm. Place each square on a lined baking tray. Put a teaspoon of cold pastry cream in the centre of each square. Make a diagonal cut from each corner to within 2 cm of the centre. Fold every other flap inward over the cream to form a pinwheel and press the tips firmly into the cream to anchor them. Place a drained apricot half in the centre, cut-side down. Brush the pastry (not the apricot) with beaten egg. Leave to prove at room temperature for 45 to 60 minutes until noticeably puffed. Preheat the oven to 200 degrees C (conventional) and bake for 18 to 22 minutes until deep golden. Brush with warmed apricot jam immediately as they come out of the oven.
